\signature{Derek Tharp\\Department of Accounting \& Finance\\University of Southern Maine\\derek.tharp@maine.edu}

\begin{document}

\begin{letter}{Editors\\Journal of Public Economics}

\opening{Dear Professors Hendren and Kopczuk,}

I am submitting ``Quantifying the Annuity Puzzle: A Unified Lifecycle Decomposition'' for consideration at the \textit{Journal of Public Economics}.

Yaari (1965) proved that rational consumers should fully annuitize under actuarially fair pricing, yet voluntary ownership among US retirees is only 3--6\%. Six decades of research have proposed partial explanations for this gap, but the literature has lacked a unified quantitative accounting of how the main channels interact in a single model. Pashchenko (2013, \textit{JPubE}) came closest, but still predicted participation of roughly 20\%.

This paper nests eleven channels within a single calibrated lifecycle framework. The results have a three-layer structure. Seven standard rational channels reduce predicted ownership from a frictionless population benchmark of \ownFrictionless{} to \ownSevenChannel{} under modern annuity pricing, consistent with the residual overprediction in Pashchenko (2013). Two preference channels (age-varying consumption needs from Aguiar-Hurst 2013 and state-dependent utility from Finkelstein-Luttmer 2013) bring the prediction to \ownNineChannel{}. Two behavioral channels then operate at distinct decision moments and point in opposite directions: source-dependent utility (Force A; Blanchett-Finke 2024, 2025) raises predicted ownership to \ownTenChannel{} by unlocking the post-purchase ``license to spend'' on annuity income, and a narrow-framing purchase penalty (Force B; Barberis-Huang 2009 narrow framing under Tversky-Kahneman 1992 loss aversion) suppresses it through loss aversion over the unrecouped premium until breakeven. The narrow-framing penalty is calibrated by single-moment SMM to the UK 2015 pension freedoms reform, the cleanest available natural experiment isolating choice architecture from price and product. Across alternative single-moment SMM specifications all anchored to the UK reform, $\psi_{\text{purchase}} \in [\pPsiUKLow{}, \pPsiUKBLow{}]$ and the corresponding predicted US ownership ranges from \ownPsiUKBLow{} to \ownPsiUKLow{}, a bracket that contains the observed HRS rate of \pctHRSObserved{}. The headline production point estimate of \ownElevenChannel{} uses the midpoint anchor (Anchor C-mid: 60 pp behavioral residual after correcting for the simultaneous tax-rule change). The model's implied default-vs-opt-in elasticity at the production point (\defaultGapPsiUKMid{} pp) is consistent with the Goda-Manchester-Sojourner federal Thrift Savings Plan natural experiment, which was not used in calibration.

The paper makes four contributions. First, it identifies age-varying consumption needs as a quantitatively important channel missing from prior annuity-puzzle models. Second, it formally incorporates two countervailing behavioral channels each calibrated out of sample from independent empirical evidence, demonstrating that the joint pattern (individually large forces with modest net effect) reflects empirically distinct cognitive processes rather than redundant parameterizations of the same wedge. Third, it provides an exact Shapley decomposition computed over all $2{,}048$ channel subsets, delivering order-independent attribution; the two behavioral channels enter with opposite-sign Shapley values, confirming non-overlap. Fourth, it identifies two distinct policy levers: supply-side (group pricing raises ownership from \ownElevenChannel{} to \ownGroupPricing) and demand-side (default architecture, modeled as $\psi_{\text{purchase}} = 0$, raises it to \ownTenChannel), operating on different margins.

I believe the paper is a good fit for the \textit{Journal of Public Economics}. The paper engages the journal's core interests in Social Security, retirement saving, insurance-market frictions, and the design of public and employer-sponsored retirement institutions. It also directly engages with Pashchenko (2013) and related work published in \textit{JPubE} and neighboring outlets.

Replication code in Julia and all calibration data are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/DerekTharp/annuity-puzzle-model}. The manuscript has not been submitted elsewhere.

\closing{Sincerely,}

\end{letter}
\end{document}
